quiz.title: Geometry: Composite Solids — Surface Area
quiz.subtitle: Calculating Surface Area of Figures Composed of Multiple Solids
quiz.subject: Geometry

questions.question: When finding the surface area of a composite solid made of two shapes joined together, what happens to the faces where the two solids meet?
options: Those faces are not included — they are interior to the solid
They are counted twice — once for each solid
Only half their area is counted
questions.explanation: The joined faces are hidden inside the composite solid and are not part of the exterior surface. When calculating total surface area, you must subtract the area of any hidden faces from the sum of the individual solids' surface areas.

questions.question: A small rectangular prism ($3 \times 4 \times 5$ cm) is centered on top of a larger rectangular prism ($8 \times 10 \times 6$ cm). What is the total surface area of the composite solid?
questions.explanation: Top prism SA $= 2(12 + 15 + 20) = 94$ cm$^2$. Bottom prism SA $= 2(80 + 48 + 60) = 376$ cm$^2$. The shared face ($3 \times 4 = 12$ cm$^2$) is hidden — subtract it twice (once from each prism's SA). Total $= 94 + 376 - 2(12) = 470 - 24 = 446$ cm$^2$.

questions.question: A cone sits perfectly on top of a cylinder. Both have radius $5$ cm. The cylinder height is $10$ cm and the cone slant height is $13$ cm. What is the total surface area in terms of $\pi$?
questions.explanation: Cylinder lateral $= 2\pi(5)(10) = 100\pi$, exposed bottom base $= \pi(5^2) = 25\pi$. Cone lateral $= \pi(5)(13) = 65\pi$. The cylinder's top base and the cone's base are hidden at the junction. Total $= 100\pi + 25\pi + 65\pi = 190\pi$ cm$^2$.

questions.question: A hemisphere sits on top of a cylinder, both with radius $4$ m. The cylinder height is $9$ m. What is the total surface area, rounded to the nearest tenth?
questions.explanation: Cylinder lateral $= 2\pi(4)(9) = 72\pi$, exposed bottom base $= \pi(4^2) = 16\pi$. Hemisphere curved $= 2\pi(4^2) = 32\pi$. The cylinder's top base and hemisphere's flat base are hidden. Total $= 72\pi + 16\pi + 32\pi = 120\pi \approx 376.99 \approx 377.0$ m$^2$.

questions.question: A square pyramid sits perfectly on top of a cube. Both have base side $6$ ft. The pyramid slant height is $5$ ft. What is the total surface area?
questions.explanation: Cube has $5$ exposed faces (top is hidden): $5 \times 6^2 = 180$ ft$^2$. Pyramid lateral $= \frac{1}{2}P\ell = \frac{1}{2} \times 24 \times 5 = 60$ ft$^2$. The pyramid's base and the cube's top face are hidden at the junction. Total $= 180 + 60 = 240$ ft$^2$.

questions.question: A cylinder of radius $6$ cm and height $15$ cm has a smaller cylinder of radius $2$ cm drilled completely through its center along the axis. What is the total surface area of the resulting solid, in terms of $\pi$?
questions.explanation: Exterior lateral $= 2\pi(6)(15) = 180\pi$. Top and bottom are now annular rings: $2 \times (\pi(6^2) - \pi(2^2)) = 2(36\pi - 4\pi) = 64\pi$. Interior lateral (the drill hole) $= 2\pi(2)(15) = 60\pi$. Total $= 180\pi + 64\pi + 60\pi = 304\pi$ cm$^2$.

questions.question: A solid consists of a square prism ($5 \times 5 \times 10$ cm) with a hemisphere of radius $5$ cm attached to one square face. What is the total surface area, rounded to the nearest tenth?
questions.explanation: Prism SA without the attached face: $250 - 25 = 225$ cm$^2$ (the $5 \times 5$ face is hidden). Hemisphere curved surface $= 2\pi(5^2) = 50\pi \approx 157.1$ cm$^2$. Total $= 225 + 157.1 = 382.1$ cm$^2$.

questions.question: Two identical cones of radius $4$ cm and slant height $7$ cm are glued together at their circular bases. What is the total surface area in terms of $\pi$?
questions.explanation: Each cone lateral $= \pi r\ell = \pi(4)(7) = 28\pi$ cm$^2$. Both circular bases are hidden at the junction. Total $= 2 \times 28\pi = 56\pi$ cm$^2$. The resulting shape is a double cone — only the two curved surfaces are exposed.

questions.question: A grain silo consists of a cylinder of diameter $12$ ft and height $20$ ft, topped by a hemisphere. What is the total surface area, rounded to the nearest square foot?
questions.explanation: Radius $= 6$ ft. Cylinder lateral $= 2\pi(6)(20) = 240\pi$, bottom base $= \pi(6^2) = 36\pi$. Hemisphere curved $= 2\pi(6^2) = 72\pi$. The cylinder's top base and hemisphere's flat base are hidden. Total $= 240\pi + 36\pi + 72\pi = 348\pi \approx 1{,}093.3 \approx 1{,}093$ ft$^2$.

questions.question: A cylindrical tank of radius $5$ ft and height $12$ ft has a conical roof of the same radius and slant height $8$ ft. The base of the tank sits on the ground. What is the total exposed surface area, rounded to the nearest tenth?
questions.explanation: Cylinder lateral $= 2\pi(5)(12) = 120\pi$. Bottom base sits on the ground — not exposed. Top base is hidden by the cone. Cone lateral $= \pi(5)(8) = 40\pi$. Total $= 120\pi + 40\pi = 160\pi \approx 502.65 \approx 502.7$ ft$^2$. Always check which surfaces are exposed and which are hidden or on the ground.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have mastered surface area of composite solids. You correctly identify hidden faces at junctions, subtract overlapping areas, and handle combinations of prisms, cylinders, cones, pyramids, hemispheres, and solids with holes.
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Remember: (1) Faces where solids join are hidden — subtract them from the total, (2) Faces resting on the ground are not exposed, (3) When a hole is drilled, the interior surface adds to the total SA, (4) Work shape by shape, then adjust for hidden faces.
scoreMessages.50: A fair start. Focus on: (1) identifying all exposed surfaces before calculating, (2) subtracting hidden faces (usually the area where solids meet), (3) adding interior surfaces created by holes or cutouts, (4) using correct formulas for lateral and base areas of each component solid.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! For composite solids: (1) Find the surface area of each component solid separately, (2) Identify faces that are hidden at junctions — subtract their area twice (once from each component), (3) Add interior surfaces from holes, (4) Check that bases on the ground are not counted. Drawing a net or labeled diagram helps avoid missing or double-counting faces.
